questions.question: What is your current citizenship?
questions.helper_text: Your citizenship determines whether you qualify for the E-2 Treaty Investor visa. Only nationals of treaty countries are eligible.
questions.tooltip: E-2 visas are only available to nationals of countries with a Treaty of Commerce and Navigation with the United States.

questions.question: Where are you in your E-2 journey?
questions.helper_text: This helps us tailor your results and next steps to where you actually are.

options.text: I have made the decision — I am ready to apply
I am seriously researching and getting close
I am exploring whether this is right for me

questions.question: When are you hoping to be living in the United States?
questions.helper_text: This helps us personalise your timeline and compliance calendar.

options.warning_message: This is an ambitious timeline. Starting immediately gives you the best chance of meeting it.

questions.question: Who is this application for?
questions.helper_text: This determines the scope of your application package and pricing.

options.text: Just me — I am the only applicant
Me and my spouse or partner — we are applying together
A group of business partners — multiple applicants

questions.question: Who is moving with you?
questions.helper_text: Your response determines E-2 dependent visa strategy and pricing.

options.text: Just me — I am moving alone

questions.question: What are your children's ages?
questions.helper_text: Dependent status only applies to unmarried children under 21. This affects timeline and compliance planning.

options.warning_message: Children aged 18–20 may age out before visa issuance. This time-sensitive risk should be addressed early.
Children 21 or older do not qualify as E-2 dependents. They will need their own visa pathway.

questions.question: Will you have a business partner or co-investor?
questions.helper_text: Partnership structure affects ownership requirements, treaty basis, and pricing. Spousal partnerships and 50/50 partnerships have different compliance implications.
questions.tooltip: E-2 principal applicants must own at least 50% of the enterprise. Partnerships require careful structuring.

options.text: No — I am the sole owner and investor

options.warning_message: Unequal partnership splits require additional documentation of control and ownership. This is flagged for attorney review.

questions.question: What is your partner's role in the business?
questions.helper_text: This affects whether your spouse qualifies for independent E-2 status and determines ownership structure documentation.

options.text: Spouse — not involved in business operations
Business partner — silent investor, no daily role
options.warning_message: Silent investors in E-2 partnerships raise questions about active management. This is flagged for review.

questions.question: Where are you currently located?
questions.helper_text: This determines whether you'll apply through a US consulate abroad (consular processing) or adjust status from within the US. If you're unsure, select 'Not sure — I need guidance' and we'll advise based on your situation.
questions.tooltip: Consular processing and change of status have different requirements, timelines, and risks. Your current location is the starting point for determining the best path.

options.text: Inside the US — I want to change status
Outside the US — I'll apply at a consulate
I have a pending COS application

questions.question: What is the primary source of your investment funds?
questions.helper_text: Select all that apply. Each source requires specific documentation to prove the funds are lawfully obtained and traceable.
questions.tooltip: USCIS requires a clear paper trail for all investment funds. Different sources require different supporting documents — selecting accurately here helps us prepare the right documentation checklist.

options.text: Sale of a property or business

options.warning_message: Gift-funded investments require additional documentation proving the gift is genuine and not a disguised loan.
Loan-funded investments require careful structuring. The loan must be secured by personal assets, not the business. We recommend attorney guidance.

questions.question: What is your estimated total investment?
questions.helper_text: E-2 investment must be 'substantial' relative to the total cost of the business. The standard benchmark is $100,000+.
questions.tooltip: The substantiality test compares your investment to the total cost of either purchasing an existing business or starting a new one. Amounts under $50,000 rarely qualify.

options.warning_message: Investments below $100k face higher scrutiny. The substantiality test becomes more demanding at lower amounts.
This range is below the typical substantiality threshold. Success requires strong justification of proportionality to total business cost.
Investments under $50,000 rarely meet the substantiality requirement for E-2 classification.

questions.question: What best describes the business you are investing in?
questions.helper_text: Different business types have different compliance profiles and documentation requirements for E-2 purposes.

options.text: Service business — consulting, staffing, cleaning, or similar
Franchise — an established brand and system
Food and beverage — restaurant, café, or catering
Real estate — with active development or management
I have not identified a specific business yet
options.warning_message: Your business must be identified before an E-2 application can be filed. Preparation can begin now — we will help you build a strong profile while you search.

questions.question: What is the approximate total cost to start or acquire this business — including purchase price, startup costs, and working capital?
questions.helper_text: This helps us assess the proportionality of your investment relative to the total business cost — a key factor in the substantiality test.
questions.tooltip: USCIS evaluates whether your investment is substantial relative to the total cost of the enterprise. A higher investment percentage strengthens your case.

Q0-business-type: Service business — consulting, staffing, cleaning, or similar
Franchise — an established brand and system
Food and beverage — restaurant, café, or catering
Real estate — with active development or management

questions.question: What is your current business situation?
questions.helper_text: This helps us understand where you are in the E-2 process and tailor your document package accordingly.

options.text: I have a specific business identified and ready to invest in
I am researching businesses and franchises
I want to start a new business from scratch
A broker or advisor introduced me to a business

questions.question: What type of business is it?
questions.helper_text: Different business types have different compliance profiles for E-2 purposes.

Q0-08: I have a specific business identified and ready to invest in

options.text: Franchise — established brand with proven model
Independent business — restaurant, retail, service, etc.
Tech or consulting — low-overhead service business
Real estate investment or holding company
options.warning_message: Passive real estate holdings generally do not qualify as E-2 enterprises. The business must be active and operating.
Non-profit organisations generally do not qualify for E-2 classification. The enterprise must be a for-profit commercial business.

questions.question: Who introduced you to the business opportunity?
questions.helper_text: Broker-introduced deals require additional due diligence documentation for E-2 purposes.

Q0-08: A broker or advisor introduced me to a business

questions.question: Have you made contact with the franchisor yet?
questions.helper_text: This helps us understand your stage in the franchise process and what documentation you may already have.

Q0-business-type: Franchise — an established brand and system

options.text: Yes — I have been in contact and have information
No — I know which franchise I want but have not reached out
I am still deciding which franchise

questions.question: Would you like us to connect you with this franchisor on your behalf?
questions.helper_text: We can facilitate an introduction to help move your application forward.

Q0-08c: No — I know which franchise I want but have not reached out

questions.question: Do you have anything to disclose about your immigration or criminal history?
questions.helper_text: This includes prior visa refusals, border incidents, deportation orders, or criminal convictions. If you're unsure, select 'Not sure — I may have something' and we'll help you assess.
questions.tooltip: Immigration history is one of the most sensitive areas of E-2 adjudication. Full disclosure is essential — undisclosed issues discovered later can result in permanent bars.

options.text: Yes, I have something to disclose
Not sure — I may have something

questions.question: Which of the following apply to your history?
questions.helper_text: Select all that apply. This helps us prepare the right documentation and legal strategy.

Q0-09: Yes, I have something to disclose
Not sure — I may have something

options.text: I have been refused a US visa
options.warning_message: Prior visa refusals must be addressed in your application. We will prepare a detailed explanation.

options.text: I have been refused entry at a US border
options.warning_message: Border refusals are serious and must be thoroughly addressed. Attorney review is recommended.

options.text: I have been deported or removed from the US
options.warning_message: Deportation or removal orders create significant legal complexity. This requires professional legal guidance.
Criminal history requires careful admissibility analysis. We recommend consulting with an immigration attorney.

questions.question: When did the visa refusal occur?
questions.helper_text: The timing of a prior refusal affects how we address it in your application.

Q0-09c: I have been refused a US visa

options.warning_message: Recent refusals require detailed explanation and evidence of changed circumstances.
Refusals within the last 5 years require careful explanation in your application narrative.
Older refusals still need to be disclosed, but carry less weight in adjudication.

options.text: I have been refused more than once
options.warning_message: Multiple refusals create a pattern that requires strong evidence of eligibility and changed circumstances.

questions.question: Please describe the nature of your criminal conviction.
questions.helper_text: The type and recency of a conviction determines admissibility and required legal preparation.

options.text: Minor offence — resolved more than 10 years ago
options.warning_message: Older minor convictions are generally less problematic but still require disclosure and documentation.

options.text: Minor offence — resolved within the last 10 years
options.warning_message: Recent convictions require careful admissibility analysis. Professional legal guidance is recommended.

options.text: I am unsure about the exact details or admissibility
options.warning_message: Uncertainty about criminal history requires professional legal review before proceeding.
Serious convictions may create admissibility bars. This requires immediate attorney consultation.

questions.question: What ties do you maintain in your home country?
questions.helper_text: Select all that apply. Strong home ties demonstrate non-immigrant intent — a critical factor in E-2 adjudication.
questions.tooltip: E-2 applicants must demonstrate they intend to return to their home country. The strength of home ties is a key adjudication factor.

options.warning_message: Limited home ties make non-immigrant intent harder to demonstrate. We will need to build a strong case around available evidence.

hard_stops.PR-LOW-INVESTMENT: Your investment amount appears below the typical threshold for E-2 classification. The treaty investor visa requires a 'substantial' investment relative to the total cost of the enterprise. Amounts below $50,000 rarely meet this standard.
hard_stops.PR-NON-TREATY: Your country of citizenship does not currently have an active E-2 treaty with the United States. Only nationals of treaty countries are eligible for E-2 classification.
hard_stops.PR-PASSIVE-INVEST: Passive investments such as stock portfolios, rental properties held for income, or other non-operational assets do not qualify for E-2 classification. The investment must be in an active, operating commercial enterprise.
hard_stops.PR-NONPROFIT: Non-profit organisations do not qualify for E-2 treaty investor classification. The E-2 visa requires a for-profit commercial enterprise.

outcomes.PROCEED: Your profile shows strong E-2 eligibility. You meet the core requirements for treaty investor classification.
outcomes.PROCEED_RISK: Your profile shows E-2 eligibility with manageable risk factors. We recommend addressing the flagged items during preparation.
outcomes.ATTORNEY_RECOMMENDED: Your profile has complexity that benefits from legal review. We recommend consulting an immigration attorney alongside your preparation.
outcomes.DO_NOT_PROCEED: Based on your responses, there are significant barriers to E-2 eligibility that should be resolved before proceeding.
